title: Latent wear-state feature extraction via state-space filtering with pit resets
summary: Recent F1 modeling work treats degradation as a latent state observed through lap times, with pit stops as state resets.
rationale: Recent F1 modeling work treats degradation as a latent state observed through lap times, with pit stops as state resets. That is a better match to warm-up, hidden pace loss, and noisy observed degradation than static ratios built from `TyreLife` and `Cumulative_Degradation` alone.
implementation_hint: Per `(Year,Race,Driver)` fit a lightweight forward-only filter on lap-time residuals after removing race-progress trend, resetting state at pit stops. Export a few features such as latent wear level, wear slope, warm-up flag after pit, and uncertainty. Feed those into the existing binary model rather than replacing it.
expected_effect: Improves detection of ‘old but still okay’ versus ‘about to cliff’ stints and separates fresh-tyre warm-up from genuine degradation.
risk: A backward smoother would leak future information; use filtering only. Also guard against overfitting because the public paper is a 2025 preprint, so the exact gains are still uncertain.
